_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 9-25R Glucagon (Revised 3/3/2025, MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template.
protocol_reference: Michigan Medication Protocol Section 9-25R: Glucagon — MDHHS Approved 3/3/2025, Reviewed 2025
indications: Hypoglycemia when IV access unavailable and dextrose indicated
contraindications: Pheochromocytoma (adrenal gland tumor) — glucagon can cause severe hypertension
precautions: Use when IV/IO access is unavailable and dextrose would otherwise be indicated
Effect is delayed compared to IV dextrose — allow 10-15 minutes for response
Glucagon requires glycogen stores to work — may be ineffective in patients with depleted glycogen (prolonged fasting, alcoholism, adrenal insufficiency)
CRITICAL: Use ONLY intranasal glucagon formulation (Baqsimi or equivalent) for IN route — standard injectable formulation is NOT for intranasal use
CRITICAL: Use ONLY injectable formulation for IM route — intranasal formulation is NOT for injection
Pediatric <5 years: IM route only; DO NOT give IN to patients <5 years
expected_effects: Increase in blood glucose via hepatic glycogenolysis
Improvement in mental status (onset 10-20 minutes IM, 10-15 minutes IN)
side_effects: Nausea and vomiting (common — position patient to prevent aspiration)
notes: Adult and pediatric ≥5 years: 1 mg IM OR 3 mg IN (using intranasal glucagon device)
Pediatric <5 years: 0.5 mg IM ONLY — DO NOT give IN
Position patient in recovery position to prevent aspiration of vomiting
Once IV access established: administer dextrose per 9-19R; glucagon is the bridge to IV access

dosing.notes: Injectable formulation only for IM route
Use ONLY intranasal-specific glucagon formulation (e.g., Baqsimi); do NOT use injectable formulation intranasally
DO NOT give intranasal to patients <5 years; IM with injectable formulation only

used_in_protocols: Hypoglycemia / Diabetic Emergencies — when IV unavailable
